perturbation.original_text: 1. TERM As provided for in this Section 1, the term of this Agreement shall be for a period of five (5) years, beginning on the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"); provided, however, the Initial Term shall be subject to automatic successive renewal terms of three (3) years each (the "Renewal Terms" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term").
perturbation.changed_text: 1. TERM As provided for in this Section 1, the term of this Agreement shall be for a period of five (5) years, beginning on the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"); provided, however, the Initial Term shall be subject to renewal terms of three (3) years each (the "Renewal Terms" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term"), subject to mutual written agreement by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that renewal terms are automatic. The modified version adds "subject to mutual written agreement by both parties," which contradicts the 'automatic' nature of the renewal. This creates an in-text contradiction, impacting the certainty of the contract's duration and creating ambiguity about whether renewal requires active consent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. TERM As provided for in this Section 1, the term of this Agreement shall be for a period of five (5) years, beginning on the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"); provided, however, the Initial Term shall be subject to renewal terms of three (3) years each (the "Renewal Terms" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term"), subject to mutual written agreement by both parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated an 'automatic' renewal term. The changed text introduces a requirement for 'mutual written agreement,' directly contradicting the automatic nature of the renewal. This change imposes a condition (mutual agreement) where none existed before, making the renewal no longer automatic and therefore inconsistent with the original intent. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, but the key point is that the 'mutual written agreement' clause now creates an in-text contradiction with the implied meaning of an automatic renewal, impacting the certainty of the contract's duration.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1. Purchase. In exchange for the exclusive distribution right and license granted to Distributor pursuant to this Agreement, during the Term, Distributor hereby agrees to purchase the Products from Vendor, where such Products are fit for purpose and ready for sale in the Territory, as determined by Distributor, as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1. Purchase. In exchange for the exclusive distribution right and license granted to Distributor pursuant to this Agreement, during the Term, Distributor may purchase the Products from Vendor, where such Products are fit for purpose and ready for sale in the Territory, as determined solely by Vendor, as follows:
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that Distributor agrees to purchase products as determined by the distributor themselves, and 'agrees to purchase'. Whereas, the new clause states that Distributor 'may' purchase, as well as the product fitness is to be determined by the Vendor. This creates an in-text contradiction between the obligations of the distributor to purchase.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1. Purchase. In exchange for the exclusive distribution right and license granted to Distributor pursuant to this Agreement, during the Term, Distributor may purchase the Products from Vendor, where such Products are fit for purpose and ready for sale in the Territory, as determined solely by Vendor, as follows:
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Distributor 'agrees to purchase' and that product fitness is determined by the Distributor, whereas the Changed Text states that the Distributor 'may purchase' and fitness is determined by the Vendor. This change introduces a direct contradiction with the original obligation. The Contradicted Text is the Changed Text in this case, but the act of changing it from the Original Text creates an In-Text Contradiction. The distributor is no longer obligated to purchase, and the vendor determines fitness. This makes the contract ambiguous and difficult to enforce, because the contradicted text directly conflicts with the distributors obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 12. LIMITATION OF WARRANTIES Vendor warrants that all Products are new and that, to its knowledge, all Vendor-supplied promotional materials comply in all respects with all applicable laws, rules and regulations. Although Vendor intends to provide a six-month limited warranty to the end user, Distributor shall make no warranties or representations with respect to the Products on behalf of Vendor. Distributor shall defend, indemnify and hold Vendor harmless from any and all claims, damages, costs or expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by Vendor in relation to any violation by Distributor of the foregoing sentence.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. LIMITATION OF WARRANTIES Vendor warrants that all Products are new. Although Vendor intends to provide a six-month limited warranty to the end user, Distributor shall make warranties or representations with respect to the Products on behalf of Vendor with Vendor's consent, but in the event Vendor does not provide any approval or denial, Distributor is free to move forward with no further approval. Distributor shall defend, indemnify and hold Vendor harmless from any and all claims, damages, costs or expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by Vendor in relation to any violation by Distributor of the foregoing sentence.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states Distributor can't make any warranties on behalf of the Vendor. The modified version now states that the Distributor 'shall make warranties' which contradict that no warranties should be made. This creates an in-text contradiction between the obligations of both parties in terms of warranties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. LIMITATION OF WARRANTIES Vendor warrants that all Products are new. Although Vendor intends to provide a six-month limited warranty to the end user, Distributor shall make warranties or representations with respect to the Products on behalf of Vendor with Vendor's consent, but in the event Vendor does not provide any approval or denial, Distributor is free to move forward with no further approval. Distributor shall defend, indemnify and hold Vendor harmless from any and all claims, damages, costs or expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by Vendor in relation to any violation by Distributor of the foregoing sentence.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited the Distributor from making warranties on behalf of the Vendor. The modified text states the Distributor 'shall make warranties,' creating a direct and irreconcilable conflict. This is a strong contradiction within the same section of the contract, regarding the obligations and permissions related to warranties. The changed text directly contradicts what was originally stated and creates a conflict that would create confusion regarding the enforcement of this clause. The explanation provided also suggests this contradiction.
